Dedication 
To the men and women across the centuries who have devoted their minds 
and
 
hearts
 
to
 
the
 
Scriptures,
 
to
 
the
 
prophets
 
and
 
apostles
 
who
 
first
 
bore
 
God’s
 
Word,
 
to
 
the
 
Church
 
Fathers
 
who
 
preserved
 
it,
 
to
 
the
 
Reformers
 
who
 
contended
 
for
 
it,
 
and
 
to
 
the
 
scholars,
 
pastors,
 
and
 
teachers
 
who
 
continue
 
to
 
wrestle
 
with
 
its
 
depths
 
and
 
proclaim
 
its
 
truth.
 
This work is offered in gratitude for their faithful labor, often unseen, often 
costly,
 
yet
 
never
 
in
 
vain.
 
May
 
their
 
diligence
 
in
 
the
 
text
 
inspire
 
us
 
not
 
only
 
to
 
study
 
the
 
Word
 
with
 
rigor,
 
but
 
to
 
behold
 
the
 
God
 
of
 
the
 
Word
 
with
 
reverence
 
and
 
joy.
 
 
2

Acknowledgement 
Writing this book has been both a journey of scholarship and of worship. I 
am
 
deeply
 
grateful
 
to
 
those
 
who
 
have
 
walked
 
with
 
me
 
along
 
the
 
way.
 
First, I give thanks to God, the Author of all Scripture and the One whose 
Spirit
 
illumines
 
its
 
pages.
 
Without
 
His
 
breath,
 
all
 
studies
 
would
 
be
 
empty,
 
and
 
without
 
His
 
grace,
 
all
 
words
 
would
 
fall
 
short.
 
I wish to acknowledge the rich legacy of biblical scholarship that has gone 
before
 
me.
 
Generations
 
of
 
faithful
 
interpreters,
 
Jewish
 
sages,
 
Church
 
Fathers,
 
Reformers,
 
theologians,
 
and
 
contemporary
 
scholars,
 
have
 
labored
 
to
 
unfold
 
the
 
treasures
 
of
 
Genesis.
 
Their
 
insights
 
have
 
shaped
 
my
 
own
 
understanding,
 
and
 
their
 
devotion
 
to
 
the
 
sacred
 
text
 
has
 
inspired
 
me
 
to
 
handle
 
it
 
with
 
care
 
and
 
reverence.
 
I am grateful to my mentors and colleagues in ministry and theology, 
whose
 
conversations
 
sharpened
 
my
 
thinking
 
and
 
encouraged
 
me
 
to
 
keep
 
pressing
 
into
 
the
 
Word.
 
To
 
my
 
family,
 
thank
 
you
 
for
 
your
 
patience,
 
love,
 
and
 
steadfast
 
support
 
during
 
long
 
hours
 
of
 
research
 
and
 
writing.
 
Finally, to every reader who longs to see the Scriptures afresh: this book is 
for
 
you.
 
May
 
it
 
stir
 
your
 
imagination,
 
ground
 
your
 
faith,
 
and
 
draw
 
your
 
eyes
 
to
 
behold
 
the
 
God
 
who,
 
in
 
the
 
beginning,
 
created
 
and
 
who
 
even
 
now
 
is
 
making
 
all
 
things
 
new.
 
 
3

Acknowledgement 3 
Preface 6 
Introduction 7

Chapter 3: "In the Beginning" - The God Who Starts and Sustains 52 ● Time, power, and intention in Hebrew worldview ● God speaks to create - the Word and Christ connection Chapter 4: A World of Order and Meaning 63 ● Structure of days 1-6: Forming and Filling ● The Spirit hovering: The Holy Spirit in creation and now in us Chapter 5: Made in His Image 73 ● Royal language and divine partnership ● Adam as prophet, priest, and king ● Christ as the true image Chapter 6: Sabbath and the Throne of God 83 ● Creation’s goal is rest, not cessation, but celebration ● God's rest as enthronement ● New Creation and the Sabbath in Christ 
 Part 3: Genesis 2 - The Garden, the Man, and the Mission 89 
Chapter 7: A Temple Called Eden 90 ● Rivers, gold, onyx, and sacred geography ● Eden as God’s dwelling place on Earth ● Adam as priest in God’s presence 4

Chapter 8: Formed from the Dust, Filled with the Spirit 97 ● The intimacy of divine creation ● The Spirit in man (Genesis 2:7 vs. John 20:22) ● Human dignity and the breath of God Chapter 9: The First Relationship 106 ● Woman as “ezer kenegdo” - powerful counterpart ● Unity, diversity, and divine image in community ● Foreshadowing Christ and His bride Part 4: From Eden to Eternity - Seeing the Bible Through Genesis 113 
Chapter 10: The Gospel in the Garden 114 ● Christ as the second Adam ● The tree of life and the cross ● The bride from Adam’s side and the church from Christ’s Chapter 11: New Creation: From Eden to the Church 120 ● The Spirit in Genesis and the Spirit in us ● The believer as a walking Eden ● The mission of multiplication and fruitfulness fulfilled in the Great Commission Chapter 12: The World is a Temple - Living with Cosmic Purpose 126 ● Vocation and worship as two sides of the same coin ● Earth as God’s holy ground ● Our lives as micro-Edens and mobile sanctuaries Chapter 13: Behold Our God 132 ● God revealed as Creator, King, Redeemer, and Dwelling One ● Creation as gift, not chaos; Sabbath as enthronement ● Beholding God as the path to becoming His image Epilogue: Back to the Beginning to See the End 138 ● Revelation’s Edenic restoration ● Heaven and Earth reunited in Christ ● Living now with Genesis eyes

Preface 
Over the years, as a pastor, I’ve noticed a recurring pattern in the life of our 
church.
 
No
 
matter
 
the
 
occasion,
 
whether
 
it's
 
our
 
Summer
 
Boot
 
Camps,
 
Winter
 
Boot
 
Camps,
 
Life
 
Conferences,
 
Word
 
&
 
Prayer
 
Conferences,
 
or
 
our
 
Easter
 
Teaching
 
Weekends,
 
I
 
often
 
find
 
myself
 
returning
 
to
 
Genesis
 
1
 
and
 
2.
 
Not
 
because
 
I
 
run
 
out
 
of
 
passages
 
to
 
teach,
 
but
 
because
 
I
 
believe
 
these
 
opening
 
chapters
 
are
 
inexhaustibly
 
rich,
 
and
 
foundational
 
for
 
everything
 
else
 
the
 
Bible
 
teaches.
 
In these verses, we are not just witnessing the beginning of time, we are 
seeing
 
the
 
mind
 
and
 
heart
 
of
 
God
 
unveiled.
 
Genesis
 
1
 
and
 
2
 
do
 
not
 
merely
 
tell
 
us
 
what
 
happened
,
 
but
 
why
 
it
 
matters
.
 
They
 
declare
 
who
 
God
 
is,
 
what
 
it
 
means
 
to
 
be
 
human,
 
and
 
how
 
everything
 
in
 
creation
 
from
 
the
 
stars
 
above
 
to
 
the
 
breath
 
within,
 
was
 
made
 
with
 
intention,
 
purpose,
 
and
 
glory.
 
I’ve come to realize that what I keep returning to privately and in teaching 
may
 
be
 
something
 
the
 
wider
 
Body
 
of
 
Christ
 
also
 
needs.
 
So
 
this
 
book
 
was
 
born.
 
It
 
is
 
my
 
effort
 
to
 
put
 
into
 
writing
 
what
 
I
 
have
 
long
 
carried
 
in
 
my
 
heart
 
and
 
proclaimed
 
from
 
the
 
pulpit,
 
a
 
robust,
 
Spirit-filled,
 
theologically
 
anchored
 
vision
 
of
 
God’s
 
original
 
design
 
and
 
eternal
 
purpose.
 
I believe Genesis 1 and 2 are not just the starting point of the Bible, they 
are
 
the
 
seedbed
 
of
 
everything
 
that
 
follows:
 
creation
 
and
 
new
 
creation,
 
image
 
and
 
identity,
 
temple
 
and
 
presence,
 
bride
 
and
 
bridegroom,
 
fall
 
and
 
redemption,
 
kingdom
 
and
 
glory.
 
My prayer is that this book will strengthen the Church, stir hunger for 
God’s
 
Word,
 
and
 
inspire
 
fresh
 
wonder
 
at
 
the
 
One
 
who,
 
in
 
the
 
beginning,
 
created
 
the
 
heavens
 
and
 
the
 
earth
 
and
 
who,
 
through
 
Christ,
 
is
 
making
 
all
 
things
 
new.
 
Welcome to the beginning. 
Dr. Daniel Folarin 
6

Introduction 
Reading Genesis as the Bible’s Opening Revelation 
When we open the Bible to the first page, we are not just reading a story, 
we
 
are
 
stepping
 
into
 
a
 
revelation.
 
Genesis
 
1
 
and
 
2
 
are
 
not
 
merely
 
ancient
 
texts
 
about
 
beginnings.
 
They
 
are
 
the
 
divine
 
prologue
 
to
 
the
 
Bible
 
itself,
 
introducing
 
us
 
to
 
the
 
God
 
who
 
creates,
 
speaks,
 
breathes,
 
blesses,
 
and
 
builds,
 
not
 
just
 
a
 
world,
 
but
 
a
 
people
 
for
 
Himself.
 
As a pastor and student of the Scriptures, I’ve come to believe that these 
opening
 
chapters
 
are
 
not
 
only
 
foundational
 
but
 
prophetic
 
and
 
revelatory.
 
They
 
are
 
not
 
locked
 
in
 
the
 
past;
 
they
 
are
 
alive
 
with
 
meaning
 
for
 
today.
 
In
 
them,
 
we
 
meet
 
a
 
God
 
who
 
is
 
personal,
 
a
 
humanity
 
made
 
with
 
purpose,
 
and
 
a
 
cosmos
 
charged
 
with
 
the
 
glory
 
of
 
its
 
Maker.
 
But how should we read Genesis? 
This question matters deeply, not just for understanding creation, but for 
understanding
 
the
 
entire
 
biblical
 
story.
 
Was Moses Writing History, Theology, or Something More? 
Most Jewish and Christian traditions affirm that Moses authored the 
Pentateuch,
 
the
 
first
 
five
 
books
 
of
 
the
 
Bible,
 
often
 
called
 
the
 
“Torah”
 
or
 
“Law.”
 
Genesis,
 
therefore,
 
is
 
traditionally
 
attributed
 
to
 
him.
 
But
 
there’s
 
an
 
obvious
 
challenge:
 
Moses
 
appears
 
for
 
the
 
first
 
time
 
in
 
Exodus
 
2,
 
long
 
after
 
the
 
events
 
of
 
Genesis
 
occurred.
 
So how did Moses know what to write? 
There are two faithful answers: 
1. He may have drawn on oral traditions or earlier records passed 
down
 
through
 
the
 
patriarchs.
 
 
7

2. He may have received direct revelation from God, particularly 
during
 
his
 
extraordinary
 
encounters
 
with
 
the
 
Divine
 
(e.g.,
 
the
 
burning
 
bush,
 
Mount
 
Sinai,
 
the
 
Tent
 
of
 
Meeting).
 
These answers are not contradictory. Together, they reflect what we see 
throughout
 
Scripture:
 
God
 
works
 
through
 
history
 
and
 
inspiration
 
to
 
reveal
 
His
 
truth
 
to
 
His
 
people.
 
Over the past 250 years, critical scholars have proposed various theories 
about
 
how
 
the
 
Pentateuch
 
was
 
composed.
 
These
 
include:
 
● Source Criticism (suggesting Moses compiled various written 
traditions
 
such
 
as
 
the
 
Yahwist,
 
Elohist,
 
Deuteronomist,
 
and
 
Priestly
 
sources)
 
● Form Criticism (studying the literary forms and social settings of 
biblical
 
texts)
 
● Traditio-Historical Criticism (tracing the development of traditions 
over
 
time)
 
● Literary Criticism (examining the text as an integrated literary 
work)
 
While some aspects of these methods can be helpful for understanding 
structure
 
and
 
background,
 
they
 
often
 
miss
 
the
 
heart
 
of
 
the
 
text.
 
The
 
Scriptures
 
are
 
not
 
merely
 
historical
 
or
 
literary
 
documents.
 
They
 
are
 
God-breathed
 
(2
 
Tim.
 
3:16),
 
given
 
to
 
reveal
 
His
 
mind,
 
His
 
will,
 
and
 
His
 
redemptive
 
plan
 
through
 
Jesus
 
Christ.
 
Genesis as the Bible Introducing Itself 
Rather than reading Genesis simply to understand the Bible, I believe we 
must
 
read
 
the
 
Bible
 
to
 
understand
 
Genesis.
 
8

Genesis is not an isolated preface. It is the seedbed of all Scripture, a rich 
soil
 
from
 
which
 
the
 
themes
 
of
 
redemption,
 
covenant,
 
kingdom,
 
and
 
new
 
creation
 
spring
 
forth.
 
In these opening chapters, we encounter: 
● A Creator who speaks life into being 
● A temple-world made to host His presence 
● A humanity bearing His image and sharing His authority 
● A rhythm of rest and communion pointing to eternal Sabbath 
● A bride prepared for the bridegroom 
● A fall that anticipates a greater restoration 
As we follow the biblical narrative from Genesis to Revelation, we begin 
to
 
see
 
that
 
these
 
themes
 
are
 
not
 
incidental,
 
they
 
are
 
intentional,
 
prophetic,
 
and
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
Christ.
 
Genesis as Christ-Centered Revelation 
This book holds a core conviction: Genesis 1 and 2 are not just about what 
God
 
did,
 
they
 
are
 
about
 
who
 
God
 
is,
 
and
 
what
 
He
 
has
 
always
 
purposed
 
in
 
Christ.
 
The Apostle John understood this when he opened his Gospel by echoing 
Genesis:
 
“In the beginning was the Word…” (John 1:1) 
Paul affirms this too: 
“By Him all things were created… all things were created 
through
 
Him
 
and
 
for
 
Him”
 
(Col.
 
1:16)
 
9

This means Genesis is not just a theological or cosmological account, it is a 
Christ-centered
 
one.
 
The Word who created is the same Word who became flesh and dwelt 
among
 
us.
 
The
 
breath
 
of
 
God
 
that
 
animated
 
Adam
 
is
 
the
 
same
 
Spirit
 
who
 
indwells
 
believers
 
today.
 
The
 
garden
 
temple
 
of
 
Eden
 
foreshadows
 
the
 
new
 
heaven
 
and
 
new
 
earth,
 
where
 
God
 
will
 
again
 
dwell
 
with
 
His
 
people.
 
A Word About Language and Audience 
It’s important to consider who Moses was writing for. The Israelites had 
just
 
emerged
 
from
 
centuries
 
of
 
slavery
 
in
 
Egypt,
 
surrounded
 
by
 
pagan
 
myths
 
and
 
distorted
 
ideas
 
about
 
the
 
gods,
 
humanity,
 
and
 
the
 
world.
 
Genesis speaks into that context with radical theological clarity: 
● There is one sovereign Creator, not many battling deities. 
● Creation is ordered, good, and purposeful, not chaotic or accidental. 
● Humans are not slaves of the gods, but image-bearers invited into 
partnership
 
with
 
God
 
Himself.
 
Could it be that Moses, inspired by the Spirit, was communicating deep 
spiritual
 
truths
 
through
 
the
 
symbols
 
and
 
structures
 
the
 
people
 
could
 
understand?
 
Absolutely.
 
And
 
like
 
the
 
parables
 
of
 
Jesus,
 
Genesis
 
offers
 
meaning
 
both
 
on
 
the
 
surface
 
and
 
in
 
the
 
depths
 
for
 
those
 
with
 
eyes
 
to
 
see.
 
How to Read This Book 
This book is written with three aims in mind: 
1. Theological clarity - I aim to show how Genesis reveals the mind 
and
 
mission
 
of
 
God.
 
10

2. Devotional insight - I invite you to encounter the God who is near 
and
 
holy.
 
3. Scholarly context - I want to offer responsible, accessible 
comparisons
 
with
 
the
 
ancient
 
world
 
to
 
highlight
 
the
 
uniqueness
 
of
 
the
 
biblical
 
message.
 
You’ll also find: 
● “Ancient Lens” offering cultural and theological comparisons with 
ancient
 
Near
 
Eastern
 
thought.
 
● “See Through Christ” showing how Genesis anticipates the person 
and
 
work
 
of
 
Jesus.
 
● Devotional Reflections, inviting you to respond personally to what 
God
 
reveals
 
in
 
each
 
section.
 
This book is not about science but about God. Still, many wonder how 
Genesis
 
relates
 
to
 
theories
 
like
 
the
 
Big
 
Bang,
 
and
 
I
 
address
 
this
 
more
 
fully
 
in
 
an
 
appendix.
 
Why Genesis 1 and 2 Still Matter 
In a world obsessed with origin debates and scientific questions, Genesis 1 
and
 
2
 
continue
 
to
 
matter,
 
not
 
because
 
they
 
answer
 
all
 
of
 
our
 
curiosities,
 
but
 
because
 
they
 
answer
 
the
 
most
 
important
 
questions
 
of
 
all:
 
- Who is God? 
- Who am I? 
- What is this world for? 
- And where is it all headed? 
The answer, from the very first verse, is this: 
11

In the beginning, God. 
And in the fullness of time, that same God came near in Christ, to redeem, 
to
 
restore,
 
and
 
to
 
bring
 
creation
 
into
 
the
 
glory
 
for
 
which
 
it
 
was
 
made.
12

Part 1: Setting the Stage - Why Genesis 1-2 Matter
13

Chapter 1 The Bible Introduces Itself 
“In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.” 
 
-
 
Genesis
 
1:1
 
More Than a Creation Myth 
When we open the Bible, we are not simply reading the past, we are 
entering
 
a
 
divine
 
unveiling.
 
Genesis
 
1
 
and
 
2
 
are
 
not
 
primitive
 
attempts
 
to
 
explain
 
the
 
natural
 
world.
 
They
 
are
 
God’s
 
self-introduction,
 
the
 
way
 
the
 
Creator
 
chooses
 
to
 
reveal
 
His
 
nature,
 
His
 
intentions,
 
and
 
the
 
role
 
of
 
humankind
 
within
 
His
 
cosmic
 
story.
 
This is not a myth in the way we usually use the word. In the ancient 
world,
 
myths
 
were
 
often
 
symbolic
 
stories
 
used
 
to
 
explain
 
the
 
universe
 
through
 
the
 
lens
 
of
 
human
 
fear,
 
tribal
 
identity,
 
and
 
the
 
capricious
 
behavior
 
of
 
the
 
gods.
 
But
 
Genesis
 
offers
 
something
 
radically
 
different.
 
It
 
is
 
revelation,
 
not
 
humanity
 
guessing
 
about
 
God,
 
but
 
God
 
speaking
 
about
 
Himself.
 
To reduce Genesis to "just another origin story" is to miss the tone and 
intent
 
of
 
the
 
text
 
entirely.
 
This
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
about
 
what
 
happened,
 
but
 
about
 
who
 
God
 
is,
 
why
 
the
 
world
 
exists,
 
and
 
what
 
it
 
means
 
to
 
be
 
human.
 
Genesis
 
doesn’t
 
begin
 
with
 
chaos
 
or
 
conflict.
 
It
 
begins
 
with
 
a
 
Word.
 
“And God said…” (Gen. 1:3) 
This phrase sets a precedent for how creation unfolds. God does not battle 
other
 
gods
 
to
 
establish
 
dominion;
 
He
 
speaks
 
and
 
His
 
Word
 
creates,
 
orders,
 
and
 
fills.
 
In
 
six
 
symmetrical
 
and
 
poetic
 
days,
 
we
 
see
 
a
 
carefully
 
structured
 
cosmos
 
come
 
into
 
being,
 
not
 
through
 
violence,
 
but
 
through
 
intentionality.
 
Time
 
is
 
ordered
 
(days),
 
space
 
is
 
ordered
 
(heavens,
 
earth,
 
seas),
 
and
 
life
 
is
 
ordered
 
(plants,
 
animals,
 
humans).
 
Creation
 
is
 
not
 
random.
 
It
 
is
 
deeply
 
personal
 
and
 
gloriously
 
structured.
 
14

This has massive theological implications. Genesis 1-2 tells us that: 
● God is One, not many. 
● God is sovereign, not struggling. 
● God is good, not malevolent. 
● Creation is very good, not an accident or a punishment. 
● Humanity is made with purpose, not as an afterthought or as slaves. 
Genesis, then, is not shaped by the worldview around it, it confronts it. 
While
 
other
 
nations
 
believed
 
humans
 
were
 
created
 
to
 
serve
 
the
 
needs
 
of
 
the
 
gods,
 
Genesis
 
declares
 
that
 
humanity
 
is
 
made
 
in
 
the
 
image
 
of
 
God
 
(Gen.
 
1:26),
 
invited
 
to
 
rule
 
and
 
cultivate
 
creation
 
alongside
 
Him.
 
This
 
is
 
not
 
just
 
different,
 
it’s
 
revolutionary.
 
Ancient Lens: The Breath of the Gods vs. the Breath of God 
In ancient Near Eastern literature like the Enuma Elish or Atrahasis, 
humans
 
were
 
often
 
formed
 
from
 
the
 
blood
 
or
 
body
 
of
 
a
 
slain
 
god.
 
The
 
"life
 
force"
 
of
 
humanity
 
was
 
seen
 
as
 
derivative
 
and
 
chaotic,
 
borrowed
 
from
 
divine
 
conflict.
 
In contrast, Genesis 2:7 offers a strikingly different vision: 
“Then the LORD God formed the man of dust from the 
ground
 
and
 
breathed
 
into
 
his
 
nostrils
 
the
 
breath
 
of
 
life,
 
and
 
the
 
man
 
became
 
a
 
living
 
creature.”
 
Here, God’s breath, not violence or divine remains, is the source of life. 
This
 
breath
 
(Hebrew:
 
neshamah)
 
is
 
personal,
 
intimate,
 
and
 
deliberate.
 
Unlike
 
the
 
impersonal
 
forces
 
of
 
myth,
 
Yahweh
 
kneels
 
over
 
His
 
creation
 
and
 
breathes
 
His
 
own
 
life
 
into
 
the
 
dust.
 
15

This concept would shape Israel’s entire understanding of what it means to 
be
 
alive,
 
not
 
merely
 
biologically,
 
but
 
spiritually
 
connected
 
to
 
the
 
very
 
presence
 
of
 
God.
 
See Through Christ: The Breath That Comes Again 
The imagery of God’s breath in Genesis doesn’t stop in the garden. It 
reappears
 
in
 
the
 
ministry
 
of
 
Jesus,
 
most
 
strikingly
 
in
 
John
 
20:22,
 
after
 
the
 
resurrection:
 
“And when he had said this, he breathed on them and said to 
them,
 
‘Receive
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit.’”
 
This act is not random, it is intentional recreation. Just as the first Adam 
received
 
breath
 
in
 
the
 
garden,
 
the
 
disciples,
 
the
 
beginning
 
of
 
the
 
new
 
humanity
 
receive
 
the
 
breath
 
of
 
God
 
again
 
through
 
Christ.
 
This
 
breath
 
is
 
now
 
the
 
Holy
 
Spirit,
 
dwelling
 
in
 
believers
 
to
 
bring
 
life,
 
power,
 
and
 
intimacy
 
with
 
God.
 
Paul echoes this in 2 Corinthians 5:17: 
“If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation.” 
Genesis 2 is not just the origin of biology, it is the template of salvation. 
What
 
God
 
began
 
in
 
Eden
 
is
 
fulfilled
 
in
 
Christ.
 
The
 
breath
 
of
 
life
 
that
 
made
 
us
 
human
 
is
 
now
 
the
 
breath
 
of
 
the
 
Spirit
 
that
 
makes
 
us
 
new.
 
Genesis is not just about beginnings; it is about destiny. What was lost in 
Adam,
 
divine
 
intimacy,
 
image-bearing
 
authority,
 
priestly
 
vocation
 
is
 
restored
 
in
 
Christ.
 
The
 
Pentecost
 
experience
 
becomes
 
the
 
global
 
Eden
 
moment.
 
The
 
Spirit
 
who
 
hovered
 
over
 
the
 
waters
 
in
 
Genesis
 
1
 
now
 
hovers
 
over
 
the
 
hearts
 
of
 
all
 
believers,
 
bringing
 
order
 
from
 
chaos,
 
light
 
from
 
darkness,
 
and
 
sonship
 
from
 
slavery.
 
“Because you are sons, God sent the Spirit of His Son into our 
hearts,
 
crying,
 
‘Abba,
 
Father!’”
 
(Galatians
 
4:6)
 
16

The breath of God is no longer exclusive to the garden, it is now poured 
out
 
on
 
all
 
flesh.
 
We
 
are
 
Spirit-carriers,
 
empowered
 
to
 
bear
 
the
 
image
 
of
 
Christ
 
and
 
walk
 
in
 
the
 
calling
 
first
 
given
 
in
 
Eden.
 
Christ in the Beginning 
The opening phrase of the Bible "In the beginning…" resonates like the 
overture
 
of
 
a
 
grand
 
symphony.
 
It
 
is
 
not
 
merely
 
a
 
timestamp;
 
it
 
is
 
a
 
divine
 
overture,
 
a
 
prelude
 
to
 
everything
 
that
 
follows.
 
These
 
three
 
words
 
don’t
 
just
 
introduce
 
time,
 
they
 
introduce
 
a
 
Person.
 
John 1:1 intentionally echoes Genesis: 
"In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, 
and
 
the
 
Word
 
was
 
God."
 
This is not just poetic parallelism. The apostle John is deliberately 
identifying
 
the
 
"Word"
 
Jesus
 
Christ
 
as
 
present
 
in
 
Genesis
 
1.
 
He
 
is
 
showing
 
us
 
that
 
the
 
creative
 
voice
 
in
 
the
 
beginning
 
was
 
not
 
merely
 
sound;
 
it
 
was
 
the
 
eternal
 
Son.
 
Christ
 
is
 
not
 
simply
 
introduced
 
later
 
in
 
the
 
New
 
Testament,
 
He
 
is
 
present
 
from
 
the
 
very
 
first
 
verse.
 
Paul reinforces this in Colossians 1:16: 
“For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, 
visible
 
and
 
invisible...
 
all
 
things
 
were
 
created
 
through
 
him
 
and
 
for
 
him.”
 
Jesus is not an afterthought or a late addition to God's plan. He is the 
architect,
 
the
 
agent,
 
and
 
the
 
aim
 
of
 
creation.

Days 4-6 fill those domains with inhabitants: 
● Day 4 : Sun, Moon, and Stars (to govern/correspond to Day 1) 
● Day 5 : Birds and Fish (to fill Day 2) 
● Day 6 : Land Animals and Humanity (to dwell in Day 3)

the

was

a

The Bible begins with a garden and ends with a garden-city. Genesis opens

To see the world through the lens of Genesis is to live with hope, vision,

Appendix: Genesis and the Question of Science 
Introduction: Two Questions, One Story 
When we open Genesis with the words “In the beginning, God created the 
heavens
 
and
 
the
 
earth”
 
(Gen.
 
1:1),
 
we
 
are
 
confronted
 
not
 
only
 
with
 
a
 
statement
 
of
 
origins
 
but
 
with
 
a
 
claim
 
of
 
sovereignty.
 
The
 
Bible’s
 
first
 
verse
 
does
 
not
 
set
 
out
 
to
 
satisfy
 
scientific
 
curiosity
 
about
 
mechanics;
 
it
 
establishes
 
that
 
the
 
world
 
has
 
a
 
beginning,
 
that
 
its
 
beginning
 
was
 
willed,
 
and
 
that
 
its
 
beginning
 
is
 
anchored
 
in
 
the
 
character
 
of
 
the
 
Creator.
 
Modern readers, however, often bring different questions to the text. “What 
about
 
the
 
Big
 
Bang?”
 
“What
 
about
 
cosmology,
 
evolution,
 
or
 
multiverse
 
theories?”
 
These
 
questions
 
are
 
not
 
new.
 
The
 
Church
 
Fathers
 
wrestled
 
with
 
Greek
 
eternalism,
 
medieval
 
theologians
 
grappled
 
with
 
Aristotelian
 
cosmology,
 
and
 
Reformation
 
and
 
post-Enlightenment
 
thinkers
 
contended
 
with
 
emerging
 
astronomy
 
and
 
geology.
 
In
 
every
 
age,
 
believers
 
have
 
asked:
 
How
 
do
 
the
 
claims
 
of
 
science
 
relate
 
to
 
the
 
witness
 
of
 
Scripture?
 
This appendix is not a comprehensive survey of every model. Rather, it 
seeks
 
to
 
set
 
Genesis
 
alongside
 
the
 
scientific
 
accounts
 
of
 
beginnings,
 
showing
 
that
 
while
 
science
 
asks
 
the
 
how
,
 
Genesis
 
reveals
 
the
 
Who
 
and
 
why
.
 
Science
 
describes
 
mechanisms;
 
Scripture
 
discloses
 
meaning.
 
And
 
when
 
both
 
are
 
rightly
 
understood,
 
they
 
are
 
not
 
enemies
 
but
 
different
 
kinds
 
of
 
witnesses
 
to
 
the
 
same
 
reality.
 
Genesis and the Beginning of Time 
The opening phrase of Scripture, “In the beginning” (Hebrew bereshit ), 
carries
 
a
 
weight
 
far
 
greater
 
than
 
a
 
chronological
 
marker.
 
It
 
announces
 
that
 
time
 
itself
 
had
 
a
 
starting
 
point.
 
In
 
the
 
ancient
 
world,
 
this
 
was
 
a
 
revolutionary
 
idea.
 
Many
 
cultures
 
conceived
 
of
 
existence
 
as
 
cyclical,
 
an
 
endless
 
loop
 
of
 
birth,
 
decay,
 
and
 
renewal.
 
The
 
Babylonian
 
Enuma
 
Elish
 
depicts
 
creation
 
as
 
arising
 
from
 
the
 
violent
 
conflict
 
between
 
primordial
 
141

deities, a battle destined to repeat as chaos always threatens order.
 1
 
Similarly,
 
Hindu
 
cosmologies
 
describe
 
the
 
universe
 
as
 
oscillating
 
through
 
unending
 
cycles
 
(
kalpas
)
 
of
 
creation
 
and
 
destruction.
2
 
In
 
these
 
accounts,
 
time
 
is
 
not
 
a
 
purposeful

but
 
an
 
eternal
 
wheel,
 
often
 
indifferent
 
or
 
hostile
 
to
 
human
 
dignity.
 
Against this backdrop, Genesis makes a bold and unparalleled claim: time 
is
 
linear,
 
purposeful,
 
and
 
moral.
 
The
 
narrative
 
does
 
not
 
begin
 
with
 
conflict
 
or

but

a
 
decisive
 
act

divine

The

determined

divinely
 
appointed

This

neighbors

lays

for
 
biblical

3

The

a

of
 
material
 
things

the

that
 
time
 
itself
 
is
 
a
 
creation,
 
dependent
 
entirely
 
on
 
God.
4

of

exists
 
in
 
eternity,
 
outside
 
of
 
time’s
 
flow.

creative
 
act,
 
Augustine
 
insists,
 
gives

to
 
time’s
 
very

About the Book. 
A Revelation from Genesis: Seeing the Whole Bible in Genesis 1 and 2 
is
 
a
 
theological,
 
devotional,
 
and
 
scholarly
 
journey
 
into
 
the
 
first
 
pages
 
of
 
Scripture.
 
More
 
than
 
an
 
introduction
 
to
 
creation,
 
Genesis
 
1
 
and
 
2
 
provide
 
the
 
foundation
 
for
 
understanding
 
the
 
whole
 
story
 
of
 
the
 
Bible.
 
Dr. Daniel Folarin guides readers through these opening chapters with 
clarity
 
and
 
depth,
 
showing
 
how
 
Genesis
 
was
 
written
 
not
 
in
 
a
 
cultural
 
vacuum
 
but
 
in
 
conversation
 
with
 
the
 
myths
 
and
 
cosmologies
 
of
 
the
 
ancient
 
Near
 
East.
 
Where
 
surrounding
 
nations
 
spoke
 
of
 
violent
 
gods,
 
endless
 
cycles,
 
or
 
humanity
 
as
 
divine
 
laborers,
 
Genesis
 
offers
 
a
 
radically
 
different
 
vision:
 
a
 
sovereign
 
God
 
who
 
creates
 
by
 
His
 
Word,
 
a
 
world
 
designed
 
as
 
His
 
temple,
 
and
 
humanity
 
dignified
 
as
 
His
 
image-bearers.
 
Drawing on biblical scholarship, historical theology, and pastoral insight, 
the
 
book
 
explores
 
themes
 
such
 
as:
 
● Creation and Order : God’s speech as the source of light, life, and 
meaning.
 
● Human Vocation : The image of God as royal-priestly calling. 
● Temple Theology : The cosmos as the dwelling place of God. 
● Christological Fulfillment : How Genesis finds its true center in 
Christ,
 
the
 
Word
 
through
 
whom
 
all
 
things
 
were
 
made
 
and
 
the
 
Redeemer
 
who
 
inaugurates
 
New
 
Creation.
 
● Practical Application : What Genesis reveals about our identity, 
purpose,
 
and
 
destiny
 
today.
 
This book is both an academic exploration and a devotional invitation. It 
bridges
 
the
 
ancient
 
and
 
the
 
modern,
 
the
 
scholarly
 
and
 
the
 
spiritual,
 
calling
 
readers
 
to
 
see
 
Genesis
 
not
 
merely
 
as
 
history,
 
but
 
as
 
revelation—a
 
168

revelation that points to Christ, centers our lives in God’s purpose, and fills 
us
 
with
 
awe
 
at
 
the
 
Creator
 
who
 
made
 
the
 
heavens
 
and
 
the
 
earth.
 
A Revelation from Genesis is for pastors, students, theologians, and 
believers
 
who
 
long
 
to
 
go
 
beyond
 
debates
 
about
 
science
 
and
 
myth,
 
and
 
instead
 
encounter
 
Genesis
 
as
 
the
 
living
 
Word
 
of
 
God.
